ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This dataset contains the production of concrete for use in building construction (strength class C 25/30) with cement Susteno (technical abbreviation according to SIA 2049: ZN/D) by Holcim Switzerland and 30% recycled aggregates from mixed demolition waste (RC-M). In its application this concrete contains reinforcement steel or other metals and is exposed to air and humidity as well as to frost-dew cycles.
Density: 2290 kg/m3, content of cement: 320 kg/m3
Exposition class according to SN EN 206: XC1, XC2, XC3 (after successful pre-testing)
Examples for this exposition class are e.g. reinforced builing elements for interior use; construction elements that are permanently submerged under water such as foundations; concrete in an outdoor environment protected from rain such as open halls or wet spaces.
Geography:  For some exchanges RER-modules have been used as proxy
Technology:  New development of a resource saving, low carbon concrete by Holcim-Lafarge Switzerland.
The cement Susteno is made of processed mixed granulate from demolition projects as a major additive. The cement Susteno reduces the clinker content of the cement and contributes to a closed material cycle. The use of recycled aggregates reduces the need for landfilling and saves primary gravel and sand resources.

This dataset was created as a weighted average (by production volumes) of the regional clinker production activities.
The dataset describes the production of portland cement clinker; in the production different types of alternative fuels and raw materials are used.
Clinker is the active component of Portland cement that, once mixed with water, contributes to the hardening of the cement itself. As a result, clinker plays a fundamental role in the production of various construction materials, including cements, concretes, and mortars. The manufacturing of clinker is highly energy-intensive and requires a significant amount of raw materials.
Large amounts of limestone, along with additional raw materials like sand, clay, iron ore, and other corrective materials—roughly 1.7 tons of these materials per ton of clinker—are thermochemically processed in order to produce clinker. Kiln ovens, as well as associated infrastructure and equipment, are needed for this process. The kiln oven must maintain high temperatures because alite, a crucial mineral in the composition of clinker that gives concrete most of its initial strength after hydration, must form at a temperature of roughly 1450°C. In order to maintain these high temperatures, a significant amount of secondary fuels (wastes) as well as conventional fuels are used to produce this energy.
The kiln technology and fuel mix are highly dependent on geographical factors.

Technology:  Clinker manufacturing begins with the collection and transportation of raw materials to the production site. The raw materials are crushed, mixed, and milled into a raw meal, which is then processed in a kiln oven. Depending on available technologies, the kiln can be either a wet or a long dry type, with the latter requiring lower energy input. Current technologies may include preheaters and precalciners, further reducing energy requirements before the raw meal enters the dry kiln.
The principal chemical reactions during pyroprocessing for clinker production are:
CaCO3 => CaO + CO2 (800°C–900°C)
2CaO + SiO2 => (CaO)2⋅SiO2 (800°C–900°C)
3CaO + Al2O3 => (CaO)3⋅Al2O3 (900°C–1300°C)

CaO + (CaO)2.SiO2 => (CaO)3⋅SiO2 (>1300°C)
Following pyroprocessing in the kiln, rapid cooling is necessary to preserve the high-temperature phase assemblage, which is thermodynamically unstable at intermediate temperatures but metastably preserved at lower temperatures due to energy and mixing barriers.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
The dataset describes the production of cement (CEM II/A-V)
CEM II/A-V is a blended cement that is made by partially replacing 6-20% of Portland cement with fly ash which is a waste from power stations.
Portland cement production involves the chemical transformation of raw materials: calcium oxides; silica; alumina and iron oxide into various types of cementitious products, by-products and wastes. The Portland cement manufacturing process consists of four main steps: (1) Quarrying of limestone and transportation of raw materials to the processing plant. (2) Preparation of “raw meal” for pyroprocessing (3) Pyroprocessing of raw materials to produce Portland cement clinker using the dry process. (4) Final grinding of the clinker together with inter-grinding with a small proportion of gypsum to produce Portland cement. Steps (1) to (4) are not considered in dataset.
Image: https://db3.ecoinvent.org/images/e16b7885-cbf8-42a4-a1e1-6a67b16f0c9f
Geography:  The dataset represents the Portland cement production for South Africa.
Technology:  In South Africa, all cement production is through dry kiln process. The clinker is ground in mills with gypsum (5%) and depending on the type of cement and technical standard (SAN 50197-1) with other materials such as blast furnace slag, fly ash, pozzolan and lime fillers to produce the Portland cement.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
The dataset describes the production of Portland cement clinker; in the production different types of alternative fuels and raw materials are used. As the use of an alternative fuel/material can vary considerably between different years, this dataset covers the period from 2017 to 2021.
The CO2 emissions from the use of alternative fuels as well as CO2 emissions from the calcination of limestone have been included in the environmental flows of fossil/non-fossil CO2 emissions. For calculations for clinker, cement and concrete that do not follow this methodological approach, recalculations have to be made adjusting the respective elementary flows in this dataset.
Geography:  This dataset represents production of clinker for Switzerland.
Technology:  Clinker consists of various calcium silicates including alite and belite. Tricalcium aluminate and calcium aluminoferrite are other common components. These components are often generated in situ by heating various clays and limestone.
Portland cement clinker is made by heating in a rotary kiln at high temperature a homogeneous mixture of raw materials. The products of the chemical reaction aggregate together at their sintering temperature, about 1,450 °C. Aluminium oxide and iron oxide are present only as a flux to reduce the sintering temperature and contribute little to the cement strength. For special cements, such as low heat (LH) and sulfate resistant (SR) types, it is necessary to limit the amount of tricalcium aluminate formed. The major raw material for the clinker-making is usually limestone mixed with a second material containing clay as source of alumino-silicate. Normally, an impure limestone which contains clay or silicon dioxide (SiO2) is used. The calcium carbonate (CaCO3) content of these limestones can be as low as 80%. Second raw materials (materials in the raw mix other than limestone) depend on the purity of the limestone. Some of the second raw materials used are: clay, shale, sand, iron ore, and bauxite.
Time period:  Time period corresponds to data collection (2017-2021).

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
Thi dataset is extrapolated from the IN corresponding dataset. Pulverized fuel ash-lime bricks are obtained from materials consisting of pulverized fuel ash in major quantity, lime and an accelerator acting as a catalyst. Pulverized fuel ash-lime bricks are generally manufactured by intergrading blending various raw materials are then moulded into bricks and subjected to curing cycles at different temperatures and pressures. These bricks are suitable for use in masonry construction just like common burnt clay bricks.
Geography:  Data represents for southern part of India,
Technology:  Fly ash, Lime, Gypsum and Sand are manually feed into a pan mixer where water is added to the required proportion for homogeneous mixing. The proportion of raw material may vary depending upon quality of raw materials. After mixing, the mixture are allowed to belt conveyor through feed in to automatic brick making machine were the bricks are pressed automatically. Then the bricks are placed on wooden pallets and kept as it is for two days thereafter transported to open area where they are water cured for 10 -15 days. The bricks are sorted and tested before dispatch.
Time period:  Data available for these period.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
Geography:  This dataset is based on data collected in 2020 from limestone crushing facilities for five (5) grey clinker cement manufacturing plants in Ontario, Canada. Current data from the Cement Association of Canada (CAC) indicates that in Ontario there are 6 cement plants, one of which is a white cement plant, not applicable to the sample. As a result, this dataset covers 100% of the production of grey clinker manufacturing in Ontario. Therefore, this dataset can be considered highly representative of the declared geographical scope, with no need for further geographical extrapolation.
The geographical overlap between Canada and Canada-Ontario for 'clinker production' is due to the high representativity of the collected data, making both datasets highly geographically representative. As a result, both datasets have been reported, with the 'Canada' dataset serving as the national average.
Time period:  The dataset covers grey cement clinker production in Canada, for the year 2020. It aggregates data from five (5) plants, averaging exchanges across various kiln technology mixes and production volumes from different suppliers. Therefore, since the reported exchanges and technology level are not anticipated to fluctuate significantly over time, the dataset has high temporal representativeness.

The dataset describes the production of portland cement clinker; in the production different types of alternative fuels and raw materials are used.
Clinker is the active component of Portland cement that, once mixed with water, contributes to the hardening of the cement itself. As a result, clinker plays a fundamental role in the production of various construction materials, including cements, concretes, and mortars. The manufacturing of clinker is highly energy-intensive and requires a significant amount of raw materials.
Large amounts of limestone, along with additional raw materials like sand, clay, iron ore, and other corrective materials—roughly 1.7 tons of these materials per ton of clinker—are thermochemically processed in order to produce clinker. Kiln ovens, as well as associated infrastructure and equipment, are needed for this process. The kiln oven must maintain high temperatures because alite, a crucial mineral in the composition of clinker that gives concrete most of its initial strength after hydration, must form at a temperature of roughly 1450°C. In order to maintain these high temperatures, a significant amount of secondary fuels (wastes) as well as conventional fuels are used to produce this energy.
The kiln technology and fuel mix are highly dependent on geographical factors.
Geography:  Activity created as a weighted average (by production volume) of the regional clinker production activities (IN, Europe without Switzerland, US, BR, ZA, CA, CO, PE, TN, EC and CH).
Technology:  Clinker manufacturing begins with the collection and transportation of raw materials to the production site. The raw materials are crushed, mixed, and milled into a raw meal, which is then processed in a kiln oven. Depending on available technologies, the kiln can be either a wet or a long dry type, with the latter requiring lower energy input. Current technologies may include preheaters and precalciners, further reducing energy requirements before the raw meal enters the dry kiln.
The principal chemical reactions during pyroprocessing for clinker production are:
CaCO3 => CaO + CO2 (800°C–900°C)
2CaO + SiO2 => (CaO)2⋅SiO2 (800°C–900°C)
3CaO + Al2O3 => (CaO)3⋅Al2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
4CaO + Al2O3 + Fe2O3 => (CaO)4⋅Al2O3⋅Fe2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
CaO + (CaO)2.SiO2 => (CaO)3⋅SiO2 (>1300°C)
Following pyroprocessing in the kiln, rapid cooling is necessary to preserve the high-temperature phase assemblage, which is thermodynamically unstable at intermediate temperatures but metastably preserved at lower temperatures due to energy and mixing barriers.
Time period:  The dataset represents a weighted average of the regional activities. The time period is set according to the time period of the regional activities.
classifications: 39310: Slag, dross, scalings and other waste from the manufacture of iron or steel

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This dataset represents the production of 1 kg clinker in Ecuador. Clinker is the main constituent of cement and the main responsible for its environmental impacts. The data is obtained for the year 2019 from the publication of Petroche & Ramirez (2022), based on information from factories.
Reference:
Petroche, D. M., & Ramirez, A. D. (2022). The Environmental Profile of Clinker, Cement, and Concrete: A Life Cycle Perspective Study Based on Ecuadorian Data. Buildings, 12(3), 311.
Geography:  This dataset is representative of the production of clinker in Ecuador.
Technology:  Current technology. Clinker is made by heating at high temperature in a rotary kiln a mixture of limestone and clays. In the production of clinker, 86% of the total raw materials are composed of limestone. Four types of clays are used, which come from different parts of 
Ecuador; these clays represent 11% by mass, and other raw materials of mineral correction 
represent 3%.
Time period:  The time period is 2019 (year of the data collection) to 2023 (year of the compilation of the dataset).
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
Geography:  This dataset is based on data collected in 2020 from limestone crushing facilities for 13 clinker cement manufacturing plants in Canada. Current data from the Cement Association of Canada (CAC) indicates that there is only one other grey cement plant in Canada that is not a CAC member, making the total number of active plants 14. As a result, this dataset covers 90% of the national production of grey clinker manufacturing in Canada. Therefore, this dataset can be considered highly representative of the declared geographical scope, with no need for further geographical extrapolation.
The geographical overlap between Canada and Canada-Ontario for 'clinker production' is due to the high representativity of the collected data, making both datasets highly geographically representative. As a result, both datasets have been reported, with the 'Canada' dataset serving as the national average.
Time period:  The dataset covers grey cement clinker production in Canada, for the year 2020. It aggregates data from 13 plants, averaging exchanges across various kiln technology mixes and production volumes from different suppliers. Therefore, since the reported exchanges and technology level are not anticipated to fluctuate significantly over time, the dataset has high temporal representativeness.
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production, average

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
The dataset describes the production of Portland cement clinker. Clinker is made by sintering a mixture composed mainly of limestone and clay at temperatures up to 1400 °C.
Primary fuels such as coal and natural gas are used along with alternative fuels are considered as a reference product (including used tyres, waste plastic, textiles, sludge, etc. ) and a waste (solid and inert waste). The dataset is mainly based on sustainability reports of three principal clinker producers of Colombia (Argos, Cemex and Holcim).
Geography:  Representative clinker production in Colombia. Data based from main clinker producers.
Technology:  This dataset represents the current technology for clinker production in Colombia
Time period:  The production volume of 2016 is considered. Data from sustainability reports of 2014 to 2017 is used. Not all the data for all exchanges was available for the same year, consequently the time period covers several years (the latest available is used).
classifications: 39270: Waste, parings and scrap of plastics
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production

parameters.comment: Literature value. Water emitted to air refers to the water that is removed from the system and thus is not available for later use (CSI consumption indicator in litres per ton of cement). According to the sustainability reports of Argos and Cemex, the share of water consumed is about 26% and 20% respectively. Weighted with the production volume, in Colombia 24% of the water withdrawn is emitted to the air and the remaining is infiltrated.
parameters.name: fraction_PW_to_air

parameters.comment: Literature value. Water emitted to air refers to the water that is removed from the system and thus is not available for later use (CSI consumption indicator in litres per ton of cement). According to the sustainability reports of Argos and Cemex, the share of water consumed is about 26% and 20% respectively. Weighted with the production volume, in Colombia 24% of the water withdrawn is emitted to the air and the remaining is infiltrated.
parameters.name: fraction_PW_to_water

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
The dataset describes the production of Portland cement clinker in Brazil. 
Clinker is the main constituent of Portland cement and also the main responsible for its environmental impacts. Clinker is made by sintering a mixture composed mainly of limestone and clay (called “raw meal”) at temperatures up to 1450 °C. Both primary and secondary fuels are used to this end. After sintering, clinker nodules have a diameter between 5 mm and 25 mm.
Clinker consists of various calcium silicates including alite (3CaO.SiO2 or “C3S”) and belite (2CaO.SiO2 or “C2S”). Tricalcium aluminate (3CaO.Al2O3 or “C3A”) and calcium aluminoferrite (4CaO.Al2O3.Fe2O3 or “C4AF”) are other common components, added to reduce the sintering temperature of the calcium silicates. For special cement types, such as low heat and sulfate resistant, it is necessary to limit the amount of tricalcium aluminate formed.
Naturally occurring calcareous deposits such as limestone, marl or chalk provide calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and quarries are often close to the cement plant. Clay, shale and sand are the preferred sources for silica. Iron ore and bauxite are the main sources of iron oxide and alumina. Raw materials are mixed at a certain proportion and are milled together in an operation called pre-homogenization, producing the “raw meal”. To ensure high cement quality, the chemistry of the raw materials and of the raw meal is very carefully monitored and controlled.
Portland cement clinker is made by heating the raw meal in a rotary kiln (calcination). As the kiln rotates, the material slides and tumbles down through progressively hotter zones towards the flame. The fuel is fired directly into the end of the kiln. The calcination reaction (CaCO3 → CaO + CO2) emits carbon dioxide into the air, and fuel combustion generates several air emissions. Different types of fuels can be used, including fossil fuels, biomass and wastes. The use of waste fuels in the cement kiln is called “coprocessing”.
Depending on the process configuration, the kiln can be preceded by preheating and/or precalcining steps. Preheating consists of a series of cyclones through which the raw meal is passed, coming in contact with swirling hot kiln exhaust gases moving in the opposite direction. The precalciner is a combustion chamber at the bottom of the preheater, at the entry of the kiln.
After the kiln, clinker is cooled and stored in silos. Clinker production plants are usually integrated with cement plants.
Primary data were gathered from six cement manufacturers in Brazil, who informed the total values of inputs and outputs related to clinker production in 2016, considering all their clinker production sites. All types of flows were reported, namely: primary and secondary raw materials and fuels, auxiliary materials, electricity consumption, water consumption and air emissions (from emissions monitoring reports). It corresponds to 70% market share, considered a good representativeness level.
SOURCES:
MEHTA, P. K.; MONTEIRO, P. J. M. Concreto: microestrutura, propriedade e materiais (Concrete: microstructure, properties and materials). 2008.
WBCSD, IEA. Cement Technology Roadmap 2009 - Carbon emissions reductions up to 2050. 2009.
Image: https://db3.ecoinvent.org/images/4e25d53a-91fb-4a9d-a433-60fd9449cdd2
Geography:  This dataset represents production of clinker for Brazil. Data come from six cement manufacturers in Brazil.
Technology:  Current technology for clinker production in Brazil, in dry kilns, considering various fuel types.
According to the GNR database, 49% of the clinker is produced in dry kilns with preheater and precalciner, being the other 51% produced in mixed kiln types (dry). Regarding fuel types, 80,5% are fossil fuels, 10,4% biomass and 9,1% alternative fossil and mixed wastes (data from 2014). According to SNIC (2013), there are 88 cement factories in Brazil. 57 are integrated plants that produce clinker and cement (ABCP, 2016), being 38 of them licensed for coprocessing.
Sources: 
SNIC (National Union of the Cement Industry). Relatório Anual 2013 (Annual report 2013). 2013.
ABCP (Brazilian Portland Cement Association). Panorama do coprocessamento - Brasil 2016 (coprocessing panorama Brazil 2016). 2016.
Time period:  Data correspond to 2016.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This dataset contains the production of concrete for use in building construction (strength class C 25/30) with cement Susteno (technical abbreviation according to SIA 2049: ZN/D) by Holcim Switzerland and 50% recycled concrete aggregates (RC-C). In its application this concrete contains reinforcement steel or other metals and is exposed to air and humidity as well as to frost-dew cycles.
Density: 2320 kg/m3, content of cement: 310 kg/m3
Exposition class according to SN EN 206: XC1, XC2, XC3
Examples for this exposition class are e.g. reinforced builing elements for interior use; construction elements that are permanently submerged under water such as foundations; concrete in an outdoor environment protected from rain such as open halls or wet spaces.
Geography:  For some exchanges RER-modules have been used as proxy
Technology:  New development of a resource saving, low carbon concrete by Holcim-Lafarge Switzerland.
The cement Susteno is made of processed mixed granulate from demolition projects as a major additive. The cement Susteno reduces the clinker content of the cement and contributes to a closed material cycle. The use of recycled aggregates reduces the need for landfilling and saves primary gravel and sand resources.
classifications: 2395:Manufacture of articles of concrete, cement and plaster
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: concrete production, 30MPa, for building construction, for interior use, with cement ZN/D, with 50% RC-C aggregates

parameters.comment: Literature value. In case when no industry/process specific data are available, default evaporation fraction value is used. This value is 15% and is based on literature (Statistics Canada, 2007 & Shaffer, K.H., 2008, Consumptive water use in the Great Lakes Basin, USGS & Shiklomanov, 2003, World Water Resources at the Beginning of the 21st Century) and has been approved by an expert.
parameters.name: fraction_PW_to_air

parameters.comment: Calculated value. See comment in the other parametres present in this dataset. Tap water is considered to be always used as process water, thus process water fractions to air are applied to all tap water input flows to calculate the amount of water consumed in the processes.
parameters.name: fraction_TW_to_air

parameters.description: fraction, water, completely softened, to air
parameters.comment: Calculated value. See comment in the other parametres present in this dataset. Water, completely softened is considered to be always used as process water, thus process water fractions to air are applied to all water, completely softened input flows to calculate the amount of water consumed in the processes.
parameters.name: fraction_WCS_to_air

Therefore, this dataset can be considered highly representative of the declared geographical scope, with no need for further geographical extrapolation.

As a result, both datasets have been reported, with the 'Canada' dataset serving as the national average.

It aggregates data from five (5) plants, averaging exchanges across various kiln technology mixes and production volumes from different suppliers. Therefore, since the reported exchanges and technology level are not anticipated to fluctuate significantly over time, the dataset has high temporal representativeness.

Primary fuels such as coal and natural gas are used along with alternative fuels are considered as a reference product (including used tyres, waste plastic, textiles, sludge, etc. ) and a waste (solid and inert waste). The dataset is mainly based on sustainability reports of three principal clinker producers of Colombia (Argos, Cemex and Holcim).

parameters.comment: Literature value. Water emitted to air refers to the water that is removed from the system and thus is not available for later use (CSI consumption indicator in litres per ton of cement). Weighted with the production volume, in Colombia 24% of the water withdrawn is emitted to the air and the remaining is infiltrated.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This dataset was created as a weighted average (by production volumes) of the regional clinker production activities.
The dataset describes the production of portland cement clinker; in the production different types of alternative fuels and raw materials are used.
Clinker is the active component of Portland cement that, once mixed with water, contributes to the hardening of the cement itself. As a result, clinker plays a fundamental role in the production of various construction materials, including cements, concretes, and mortars. The manufacturing of clinker is highly energy-intensive and requires a significant amount of raw materials.
Large amounts of limestone, along with additional raw materials like sand, clay, iron ore, and other corrective materials—roughly 1.7 tons of these materials per ton of clinker—are thermochemically processed in order to produce clinker. Kiln ovens, as well as associated infrastructure and equipment, are needed for this process. The kiln oven must maintain high temperatures because alite, a crucial mineral in the composition of clinker that gives concrete most of its initial strength after hydration, must form at a temperature of roughly 1450°C. In order to maintain these high temperatures, a significant amount of secondary fuels (wastes) as well as conventional fuels are used to produce this energy.
The kiln technology and fuel mix are highly dependent on geographical factors.
Geography:  Activity created as a weighted average (by production volume) of the regional clinker production activities (IN, Europe without Switzerland, US, BR, ZA, CA, CO, PE, TN, EC and CH).
Technology:  Clinker manufacturing begins with the collection and transportation of raw materials to the production site. The raw materials are crushed, mixed, and milled into a raw meal, which is then processed in a kiln oven. Depending on available technologies, the kiln can be either a wet or a long dry type, with the latter requiring lower energy input. Current technologies may include preheaters and precalciners, further reducing energy requirements before the raw meal enters the dry kiln.
The principal chemical reactions during pyroprocessing for clinker production are:
CaCO3 => CaO + CO2 (800°C–900°C)
2CaO + SiO2 => (CaO)2⋅SiO2 (800°C–900°C)
3CaO + Al2O3 => (CaO)3⋅Al2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
4CaO + Al2O3 + Fe2O3 => (CaO)4⋅Al2O3⋅Fe2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
CaO + (CaO)2.SiO2 => (CaO)3⋅SiO2 (>1300°C)
Following pyroprocessing in the kiln, rapid cooling is necessary to preserve the high-temperature phase assemblage, which is thermodynamically unstable at intermediate temperatures but metastably preserved at lower temperatures due to energy and mixing barriers.
Time period:  The dataset represents a weighted average of the regional activities. The time period is set according to the time period of the regional activities.
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
The dataset describes the production of cement (CEM II/B) in Switzerland. It covers the representative production mix of CEM II/B-LL, CEM II/B-M (S-T), CEM II/B-M (T-LL), CEM II/B-M (V-LL) and CEM II/B-T according to SN EN 197-1 as well as ZN/D in conformity with SIA 2049.
In Switzerland, according to the Swiss standard SN EN 197-1:2011, cement CEM II, also known as Portland composite cement, has 65-94% of clinker and is mixed with different materials, such as limestone, ground granulated blast furnace slag, fly ash, pozzolan, burnt shale, silica fume, among others. 
Geography:  This dataset represents production of cement CEM II/B for Switzerland.
Technology:  In this dataset, the production of CEM II/B is described, where clinker (69.9%) is ground together with gypsum (3.89%) and mixed with limestone (15.3%), hard coal ash, burnt shale, waste mixed aggregates (from demolition), blast furnace slag and ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBFS). The activity includes also the input of electricity used for the grinding of the clinker and grinding aids (ethylene glycol and iron sulfate).
Time period:  Time period corresponds to data collection (2017-2021).

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
The dataset describes the production of Portland cement clinker in Brazil. 
Clinker is the main constituent of Portland cement and also the main responsible for its environmental impacts. Clinker is made by sintering a mixture composed mainly of limestone and clay (called “raw meal”) at temperatures up to 1450 °C. Both primary and secondary fuels are used to this end. After sintering, clinker nodules have a diameter between 5 mm and 25 mm.
Clinker consists of various calcium silicates including alite (3CaO.SiO2 or “C3S”) and belite (2CaO.SiO2 or “C2S”). Tricalcium aluminate (3CaO.Al2O3 or “C3A”) and calcium aluminoferrite (4CaO.Al2O3.Fe2O3 or “C4AF”) are other common components, added to reduce the sintering temperature of the calcium silicates. For special cement types, such as low heat and sulfate resistant, it is necessary to limit the amount of tricalcium aluminate formed.
Naturally occurring calcareous deposits such as limestone, marl or chalk provide calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and quarries are often close to the cement plant. Clay, shale and sand are the preferred sources for silica. Iron ore and bauxite are the main sources of iron oxide and alumina. Raw materials are mixed at a certain proportion and are milled together in an operation called pre-homogenization, producing the “raw meal”. To ensure high cement quality, the chemistry of the raw materials and of the raw meal is very carefully monitored and controlled.
Portland cement clinker is made by heating the raw meal in a rotary kiln (calcination). As the kiln rotates, the material slides and tumbles down through progressively hotter zones towards the flame. The fuel is fired directly into the end of the kiln. The calcination reaction (CaCO3 → CaO + CO2) emits carbon dioxide into the air, and fuel combustion generates several air emissions. Different types of fuels can be used, including fossil fuels, biomass and wastes. The use of waste fuels in the cement kiln is called “coprocessing”.
Depending on the process configuration, the kiln can be preceded by preheating and/or precalcining steps. Preheating consists of a series of cyclones through which the raw meal is passed, coming in contact with swirling hot kiln exhaust gases moving in the opposite direction. The precalciner is a combustion chamber at the bottom of the preheater, at the entry of the kiln.
After the kiln, clinker is cooled and stored in silos. Clinker production plants are usually integrated with cement plants.
Primary data were gathered from six cement manufacturers in Brazil, who informed the total values of inputs and outputs related to clinker production in 2016, considering all their clinker production sites. All types of flows were reported, namely: primary and secondary raw materials and fuels, auxiliary materials, electricity consumption, water consumption and air emissions (from emissions monitoring reports). It corresponds to 70% market share, considered a good representativeness level.
SOURCES:
MEHTA, P. K.; MONTEIRO, P. J. M. Concreto: microestrutura, propriedade e materiais (Concrete: microstructure, properties and materials). 2008.
WBCSD, IEA. Cement Technology Roadmap 2009 - Carbon emissions reductions up to 2050. 2009.
Image: https://db3.ecoinvent.org/images/4e25d53a-91fb-4a9d-a433-60fd9449cdd2
Geography:  This dataset represents production of clinker for Brazil. Data come from six cement manufacturers in Brazil.
Technology:  Current technology for clinker production in Brazil, in dry kilns, considering various fuel types.
According to the GNR database, 49% of the clinker is produced in dry kilns with preheater and precalciner, being the other 51% produced in mixed kiln types (dry). Regarding fuel types, 80,5% are fossil fuels, 10,4% biomass and 9,1% alternative fossil and mixed wastes (data from 2014). According to SNIC (2013), there are 88 cement factories in Brazil. 57 are integrated plants that produce clinker and cement (ABCP, 2016), being 38 of them licensed for coprocessing.
Sources: 
SNIC (National Union of the Cement Industry). Relatório Anual 2013 (Annual report 2013). 2013.
ABCP (Brazilian Portland Cement Association). Panorama do coprocessamento - Brasil 2016 (coprocessing panorama Brazil 2016). 2016.
Time period:  Data correspond to 2016.
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production

Depending on available technologies, the kiln can be either a wet or a long dry type, with the latter requiring lower energy input. Current technologies may include preheaters and precalciners, further reducing energy requirements before the raw meal enters the dry kiln.
Following pyroprocessing in the kiln, rapid cooling is necessary to preserve the high-temperature phase assemblage, which is thermodynamically unstable at intermediate temperatures but metastably preserved at lower temperatures due to energy and mixing barriers.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This dataset was created as a weighted average (by production volumes) of the regional clinker production activities.
The dataset describes the production of portland cement clinker; in the production different types of alternative fuels and raw materials are used.
Clinker is the active component of Portland cement that, once mixed with water, contributes to the hardening of the cement itself. As a result, clinker plays a fundamental role in the production of various construction materials, including cements, concretes, and mortars. The manufacturing of clinker is highly energy-intensive and requires a significant amount of raw materials.
Large amounts of limestone, along with additional raw materials like sand, clay, iron ore, and other corrective materials—roughly 1.7 tons of these materials per ton of clinker—are thermochemically processed in order to produce clinker. Kiln ovens, as well as associated infrastructure and equipment, are needed for this process. The kiln oven must maintain high temperatures because alite, a crucial mineral in the composition of clinker that gives concrete most of its initial strength after hydration, must form at a temperature of roughly 1450°C. In order to maintain these high temperatures, a significant amount of secondary fuels (wastes) as well as conventional fuels are used to produce this energy.
The kiln technology and fuel mix are highly dependent on geographical factors.
Geography:  Activity created as a weighted average (by production volume) of the regional clinker production activities (IN, Europe without Switzerland, US, BR, ZA, CA, CO, PE, TN, EC and CH).
Technology:  Clinker manufacturing begins with the collection and transportation of raw materials to the production site. The raw materials are crushed, mixed, and milled into a raw meal, which is then processed in a kiln oven. Depending on available technologies, the kiln can be either a wet or a long dry type, with the latter requiring lower energy input. Current technologies may include preheaters and precalciners, further reducing energy requirements before the raw meal enters the dry kiln.
The principal chemical reactions during pyroprocessing for clinker production are:
CaCO3 => CaO + CO2 (800°C–900°C)
2CaO + SiO2 => (CaO)2⋅SiO2 (800°C–900°C)
3CaO + Al2O3 => (CaO)3⋅Al2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
4CaO + Al2O3 + Fe2O3 => (CaO)4⋅Al2O3⋅Fe2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
CaO + (CaO)2.SiO2 => (CaO)3⋅SiO2 (>1300°C)
Following pyroprocessing in the kiln, rapid cooling is necessary to preserve the high-temperature phase assemblage, which is thermodynamically unstable at intermediate temperatures but metastably preserved at lower temperatures due to energy and mixing barriers.
Time period:  The dataset represents a weighted average of the regional activities. The time period is set according to the time period of the regional activities.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This dataset contains the production of concrete for use in building construction (strength class C 20/25) with cement Susteno (technical abbreviation according to SIA 2049: ZN/D) by Holcim Switzerland and 30% recycled aggregates from mixed demolition waste (RC-M). In its application this concrete contains reinforcement steel or other metals and is exposed to air and humidity.
Density: 2297 kg/m3, content of cement: 310 kg/m3
Exposition class according to SN EN 206: XC1 dry
Examples for this exposition class are e.g. reinforced builing elements for interior use.
Geography:  For some exchanges RER-modules have been used as proxy
Technology:  New development of a resource saving, low carbon concrete by Holcim-Lafarge Switzerland.
The cement Susteno is made of processed mixed granulate from demolition projects as a major additive. The cement Susteno reduces the clinker content of the cement and contributes to a closed material cycle. The use of recycled aggregates reduces the need for landfilling and saves primary gravel and sand resources.
classifications: 2395:Manufacture of articles of concrete, cement and plaster

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
Hardening is assumed to be by air-drying. It is assumed that there is wastewater and solid waste, but as no exact description on the composition of the wastewater and solid waste is available a proxy for each ("treatment, concrete production effluent, to wastewater treatment, class 3" and "disposal, municipal solid waste, 22.9% water, to municipal incineration") is used. The lifespan of the plant is assumed to be 50 years.
Autoclaved Aerated Concrete is a Lightweight, Load-bearing, High-insulating, Durable building product, which is produced in a wide range of sizes and strengths. AAC Blocks is lightweight and compare to the red bricks AAC blocks are three times lighter.
The Autoclaved Aerated Concrete (AAC) material was developed in 1924 in Sweden. It has become one of the most used building materials in Europe and is rapidly growing in many other countries around the world.
AAC is produced from the common materials lime, sand, cement and water, and a small amount of rising agent. After mixing and molding, it is then autoclaved under heat and pressure to create its unique properties. AAC has excellent thermal insulation and acoustic absorption properties. AAC is fire and pest resistant, and is economically and environmentally superior to the more traditional structural building materials such as concrete, wood, brick and stone.
AAC is a highly thermally insulating concrete-based material used for both internal and external construction. Besides AAC's insulating capability, one of its advantages in construction is its quick and easy installation, because the material can be routed, sanded, or cut to size on site using standard carbon steel power tools.
AAC is well suited for urban areas with high rise buildings and those with high temperature variations. Due to its lower density, high rise buildings constructed using AAC require less steel and concrete for structural members. The requirement of mortar for laying of AAC blocks is reduced due to the lower number of joints. Similarly, the material required for rendering is also lower due to the dimensional accuracy of AAC. The increased thermal efficiency of AAC makes it suitable for use in areas with extreme temperatures, as it eliminates the need for separate materials for construction and insulation, leading to faster construction and cost savings.
Geography:  This dataset was built as a weighted average of regional (India and Switzerland) datasets of the same activityName, and it is assumed valid for the global production. Uncertainty has been adjusted accordingly.
Technology:  The raw materials (sand/gravel, quicklime, anhydrite, cement and aluminium powder) are mixed with water and poured into a mould. The aluminium powder is reacting  with the alcaline and siliceous additives and forms, by hydrogen release, pores with a diameter between 2 and 3mm.
classifications: 2395:Manufacture of articles of concrete, cement and plaster

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
The dataset describes the production of Portland cement clinker in Brazil. 
Clinker is the main constituent of Portland cement and also the main responsible for its environmental impacts. Clinker is made by sintering a mixture composed mainly of limestone and clay (called “raw meal”) at temperatures up to 1450 °C. Both primary and secondary fuels are used to this end. After sintering, clinker nodules have a diameter between 5 mm and 25 mm.
Clinker consists of various calcium silicates including alite (3CaO.SiO2 or “C3S”) and belite (2CaO.SiO2 or “C2S”). Tricalcium aluminate (3CaO.Al2O3 or “C3A”) and calcium aluminoferrite (4CaO.Al2O3.Fe2O3 or “C4AF”) are other common components, added to reduce the sintering temperature of the calcium silicates. For special cement types, such as low heat and sulfate resistant, it is necessary to limit the amount of tricalcium aluminate formed.
Naturally occurring calcareous deposits such as limestone, marl or chalk provide calcium carbonate (CaCO3) and quarries are often close to the cement plant. Clay, shale and sand are the preferred sources for silica. Iron ore and bauxite are the main sources of iron oxide and alumina. Raw materials are mixed at a certain proportion and are milled together in an operation called pre-homogenization, producing the “raw meal”. To ensure high cement quality, the chemistry of the raw materials and of the raw meal is very carefully monitored and controlled.
Portland cement clinker is made by heating the raw meal in a rotary kiln (calcination). As the kiln rotates, the material slides and tumbles down through progressively hotter zones towards the flame. The fuel is fired directly into the end of the kiln. The calcination reaction (CaCO3 → CaO + CO2) emits carbon dioxide into the air, and fuel combustion generates several air emissions. Different types of fuels can be used, including fossil fuels, biomass and wastes. The use of waste fuels in the cement kiln is called “coprocessing”.
Depending on the process configuration, the kiln can be preceded by preheating and/or precalcining steps. Preheating consists of a series of cyclones through which the raw meal is passed, coming in contact with swirling hot kiln exhaust gases moving in the opposite direction. The precalciner is a combustion chamber at the bottom of the preheater, at the entry of the kiln.
After the kiln, clinker is cooled and stored in silos. Clinker production plants are usually integrated with cement plants.
Primary data were gathered from six cement manufacturers in Brazil, who informed the total values of inputs and outputs related to clinker production in 2016, considering all their clinker production sites. All types of flows were reported, namely: primary and secondary raw materials and fuels, auxiliary materials, electricity consumption, water consumption and air emissions (from emissions monitoring reports). It corresponds to 70% market share, considered a good representativeness level.
SOURCES:
MEHTA, P. K.; MONTEIRO, P. J. M. Concreto: microestrutura, propriedade e materiais (Concrete: microstructure, properties and materials). 2008.
WBCSD, IEA. Cement Technology Roadmap 2009 - Carbon emissions reductions up to 2050. 2009.
Image: https://db3.ecoinvent.org/images/4e25d53a-91fb-4a9d-a433-60fd9449cdd2
Geography:  This dataset represents production of clinker for Brazil. Data come from six cement manufacturers in Brazil.
Technology:  Current technology for clinker production in Brazil, in dry kilns, considering various fuel types.
According to the GNR database, 49% of the clinker is produced in dry kilns with preheater and precalciner, being the other 51% produced in mixed kiln types (dry). Regarding fuel types, 80,5% are fossil fuels, 10,4% biomass and 9,1% alternative fossil and mixed wastes (data from 2014). According to SNIC (2013), there are 88 cement factories in Brazil. 57 are integrated plants that produce clinker and cement (ABCP, 2016), being 38 of them licensed for coprocessing.
Sources: 
SNIC (National Union of the Cement Industry). Relatório Anual 2013 (Annual report 2013). 2013.
ABCP (Brazilian Portland Cement Association). Panorama do coprocessamento - Brasil 2016 (coprocessing panorama Brazil 2016). 2016.
Time period:  Data correspond to 2016.
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
Geography:  This dataset is based on data collected in 2020 from limestone crushing facilities for 13 clinker cement manufacturing plants in Canada. Current data from the Cement Association of Canada (CAC) indicates that there is only one other grey cement plant in Canada that is not a CAC member, making the total number of active plants 14. As a result, this dataset covers 90% of the national production of grey clinker manufacturing in Canada. Therefore, this dataset can be considered highly representative of the declared geographical scope, with no need for further geographical extrapolation.
The geographical overlap between Canada and Canada-Ontario for 'clinker production' is due to the high representativity of the collected data, making both datasets highly geographically representative. As a result, both datasets have been reported, with the 'Canada' dataset serving as the national average.
Time period:  The dataset covers grey cement clinker production in Canada, for the year 2020. It aggregates data from 13 plants, averaging exchanges across various kiln technology mixes and production volumes from different suppliers. Therefore, since the reported exchanges and technology level are not anticipated to fluctuate significantly over time, the dataset has high temporal representativeness.
classifications: 39310: Slag, dross, scalings and other waste from the manufacture of iron or steel

Geography:  This dataset represents production of clinker for the region Europe without Switzerland.
Technology:  Clinker manufacturing begins with the collection and transportation of raw materials to the production site. The raw materials are crushed, mixed, and milled into a raw meal, which is then processed in a kiln oven. Depending on available technologies, the kiln can be either a wet or a long dry type, with the latter requiring lower energy input. Current technologies may include preheaters and precalciners, further reducing energy requirements before the raw meal enters the dry kiln.
Following pyroprocessing in the kiln, rapid cooling is necessary to preserve the high-temperature phase assemblage, which is thermodynamically unstable at intermediate temperatures but metastably preserved at lower temperatures due to energy and mixing barriers.

The dataset describes the production of cement (CEM II/B) in Switzerland. It covers the representative production mix of CEM II/B-LL, CEM II/B-M (S-T), CEM II/B-M (T-LL), CEM II/B-M (V-LL) and CEM II/B-T according to SN EN 197-1 as well as ZN/D in conformity with SIA 2049.
In Switzerland, according to the Swiss standard SN EN 197-1:2011, cement CEM II, also known as Portland composite cement, has 65-94% of clinker and is mixed with different materials, such as limestone, ground granulated blast furnace slag, fly ash, pozzolan, burnt shale, silica fume, among others. 
Geography:  This dataset represents production of cement CEM II/B for Switzerland.
Technology:  In this dataset, the production of CEM II/B is described, where clinker (69.9%) is ground together with gypsum (3.89%) and mixed with limestone (15.3%), hard coal ash, burnt shale, waste mixed aggregates (from demolition), blast furnace slag and ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBFS). The activity includes also the input of electricity used for the grinding of the clinker and grinding aids (ethylene glycol and iron sulfate).
Time period:  Time period corresponds to data collection (2017-2021).

Examples for this exposition class are e.g. reinforced builing elements for interior use; construction elements that are permanently submerged under water such as foundations; horizontal and vertical building elements for exterior use exposed to direct weathering (rain, frost, deicing agents (as spray mist or as spray water) such as lightning masts, balconys; concrete in an outdoor environment protected from rain such as open halls or wet spaces.

Technology:  New development of a resource saving, low carbon concrete by Holcim-Lafarge Switzerland.
The cement Susteno is made of processed mixed granulate from demolition projects as a major additive. The cement Susteno reduces the clinker content of the cement and contributes to a closed material cycle. The use of recycled aggregates reduces the need for landfilling and saves primary gravel and sand resources.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This activity represents the manufacturing of 1 kg of General Use Limestone (GUL) cement in Canada.
Cement can be defined as a finely ground, inorganic powder that, once mixed with water, undergoes a chemical reaction known as hydration. This reaction allows cement to harden and set, acting as a binder for other materials it is mixed with. This bonding and progressive hardening allow the hydrated mix to become a cohesive solid. Within the construction sector, cement refers to materials originating from calcareous rocks, such as limestone, as they provide the necessary binding properties to bind and join stones, bricks, and other construction materials. The most common type of cement is Portland cement, made primarily from limestone, clay, and other raw materials, after their pyroprocessing in kiln ovens at high temperatures (> 1400 °C) to form clinker. The manufacturing of Portland cement then starts with the grinding of clinker with gypsum into a fine powder to produce cement. Gypsum is added to control the setting of cement and avoid unwanted hydration of the clinker.
Cement finds several applications in the construction sector. The most common application is for the manufacturing of concrete, a composite mix of cement, aggregates, and water used as a structural component in buildings, pavements, and infrastructures. Additionally, cement can be used for the production of mortars, plasters, and grouts.
In this dataset, the manufacturing of "General Use Limestone" (GUL) cement is taken into account. By "general use limestone cement" or "GUL cement," it is meant a specific type of Portland cement regulated under the Canadian Standards Association (CSA) A3001 intended for general construction purposes. This cement formulation is designed to meet broad needs for a large variety of applications, from precast concrete elements to mortar manufacturing.
As specified in CSA A3001, GUL cement is a type of Portland-Limestone cement containing between 5% and 15% limestone by mass, including around 5% gypsum/anhydrite, and the rest is clinker. GUL cement for CSA A3001 corresponds to type IL cement under the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) C595 standard (USA) and it has a limited comparability with the EN 197-1 standard (Europe). This is because limestone can be used at higher percentages in CEM II cements under EN 197-1 and it has six different designations: CEM II/A-L and CEM II/A-LL cements contain between 6% and 20% limestone (by mass) while CEM II/B-L and CEM II/B-LL cements contain between 21% and 35% limestone.
Relevant literature:
Hewlett, P. C., & Liska, M. (Eds.). (2019). Lea's Chemistry of Cement and Concrete (5th ed.). Butterworth-Heinemann. https://doi.org/10.1016/C2013-0-19325-7
Geography:  This dataset is based on data collected in 2020 from 13 integrated cement plants in Canada. Current data from the Cement Association of Canada (CAC) indicates that there is only one other grey cement plant in Canada that is not a CAC member, making the total number of active plants 14. Based on production volumes, this dataset covers 90% of the national production of limestone for GUL cement manufacturing in Canada. Therefore, this dataset can be considered highly representative of the declared geographical scope, with no need for further geographical extrapolation.
The geographical overlap between Canada and Canada-Ontario for 'cement production, general use limestone (GUL)" is due to the high representativeness of the collected data, making both datasets highly geographically representative. As a result, both datasets have been reported, with the 'Canada' dataset serving as the national average.
Technology:  The cement production process begins upon receiving the clinker, which is then mixed and treated with other materials, such as gypsum and limestone, to produce General Use Limestone (GUL) cement. Below is a description of the main processes included in the database for the Canada reference geography, based on manufacturing processes surveyed in 2020.
1. Addition of gypsum and limestone
Several forms of sulfate are found in cement at the cement mill, usually in the form of gypsum, anhydrite, or a combination of both. Sulfate (SO₃) is used to control the setting of the cement. An appropriate mix of gypsum and anhydrite is necessary because CaSO₄·2H₂O will dehydrate to some extent in the heat of the cement mill, forming hemi-hydrate, also known as plaster of Paris. Excessive amounts of hemi-hydrate can cause a false (plaster) set, but a small amount is beneficial as it enhances the solution of SO₃, which is crucial for preventing the flash set of tricalcium aluminate (3CaO·Al₂O₃ or C₃A, also known as Celite, in cement's chemistry), the component of cement clinker that reacts most quickly with water.
Limestone addition is important in cement manufacturing because it helps fill the voids left by grinding clinker. Limestone grinds more easily than clinker, and, together with the gypsum, which also grinds more easily, it provides a very fine material that fills in the spaces and enhances the workability of the cement. Consequently, the addition of limestone can improve the workability of cement. Most standards permit up to 5% limestone in basic Portland cement, however CSA A3001 allows the use of limestone up to a maximum of 15% for GUL cements, which is aimed at reducing clinker content while maintaining comparability of performance with conventional portland cement (GU).
2. Milling
During the grinding process, the meal mix is ground using either vertical roller mills or horizontal ball mills. The ball mill is a rotating drum filled with steel balls that crush the meal into a fine powder through the circular movement and crushing force generated by the balls during drum rotation. In contrast, vertical mills use pressure and shear between rollers and a rotating table to crush and grind the meal. This dataset specifically considers a ball mill for the grinding process.
Additionally, various additives can be used to facilitate cement grinding. Cement grinding aids are additives introduced during the dry fine grinding process to enhance the performance of the grinding operation. They serve multiple functions, including improving grinding efficiency, increasing mill throughput, and ensuring better particle size distribution. In this dataset, ethylene glycol is used as a proxy for liquid grinding aids, while stearic acid serves as a proxy for solid grinding aids.
Source: P. Prziwara, A. Kwade, Grinding aids for dry fine grinding processes – Part I: Mechanism of action and lab-scale grinding, Powder Technol. 375 (2020). 146–160. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.powtec.2020.07.038
3. Transportation and storage
Following grinding, bucket elevators transport the finely ground cement to an air cyclone separator. The separator separates coarser particles, which are redirected back to the milling machinery, from finer particles, which are collected by fans for cement storage. The fan system includes an electrostatic precipitator (ESP) dust collector for the milling process and bag filters for collecting cement dust from the cyclone separator. The cement is then stored in silos until it is ready to be packed.
Final notes
Energy required for the operation of machinery is accounted for in this dataset. Please note that the machinery included in the dataset is also detailed in the corresponding infrastructure exchange. This includes equipment for cement recirculation, screening, hydrocyclone separation, electrostatic precipitators, and cement dust collection, as well as conveyor belts, bucket elevators and storage silos. Therefore, no other technological extrapolations are necessary, and the dataset is highly representative of the technology currently available for the region examined.
However, due to the absence of data, packaging and associated machinery for cement are not considered in this dataset or in the infrastructure intermediary exchange.
Time period:  The dataset covers GUL cement production in Canada, for the year 2020. It aggregates data from 13 out of 14 plants, averaging exchanges across various technology mixes and production volumes from different suppliers. Therefore, since the reported exchanges and technology level are not anticipated to fluctuate significantly over time, the dataset has high temporal representativeness.
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: cement production, general use limestone (GUL), average

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
The dataset describes the production of Portland cement clinker; in the production different types of alternative fuels and raw materials are used. As the use of an alternative fuel/material can vary considerably between different years, this dataset covers the period from 2017 to 2021.
The CO2 emissions from the use of alternative fuels as well as CO2 emissions from the calcination of limestone have been included in the environmental flows of fossil/non-fossil CO2 emissions. For calculations for clinker, cement and concrete that do not follow this methodological approach, recalculations have to be made adjusting the respective elementary flows in this dataset.
Geography:  This dataset represents production of clinker for Switzerland.
Technology:  Clinker consists of various calcium silicates including alite and belite. Tricalcium aluminate and calcium aluminoferrite are other common components. These components are often generated in situ by heating various clays and limestone.
Portland cement clinker is made by heating in a rotary kiln at high temperature a homogeneous mixture of raw materials. The products of the chemical reaction aggregate together at their sintering temperature, about 1,450 °C. Aluminium oxide and iron oxide are present only as a flux to reduce the sintering temperature and contribute little to the cement strength. For special cements, such as low heat (LH) and sulfate resistant (SR) types, it is necessary to limit the amount of tricalcium aluminate formed. The major raw material for the clinker-making is usually limestone mixed with a second material containing clay as source of alumino-silicate. Normally, an impure limestone which contains clay or silicon dioxide (SiO2) is used. The calcium carbonate (CaCO3) content of these limestones can be as low as 80%. Second raw materials (materials in the raw mix other than limestone) depend on the purity of the limestone. Some of the second raw materials used are: clay, shale, sand, iron ore, and bauxite.
Time period:  Time period corresponds to data collection (2017-2021).
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This inventory describes the supply of nutrients from ''thomas meal'' for fertiliser use. 
No machinery expenditures or emissions to the environment are considered related to the actual application of this substance on soil during its agricultural use. The purpose of this activity is to connect the chemical production of ''thomas meal'' with its generic use as fertilizer. The product ''thomas meal'' is connected to the generic market for 'inorganic phosphorus fertiliser, as P2O5' through this supplying activity. In the agricultural context, ''thomas meal'' can be used directly as a fertilizer input to crop production, eventually with other chemicals; alternatively, a generic input of 'inorganic phosphorus fertiliser, as P2O5' can be used instead, and this will represent the regional consumption mix for his type of nutrient. A way in between is allowed by using the generic input 'inorganic phosphorus fertiliser, as P2O5' and link to the activity providing it from a specific chemical.
 In all cases, emissions associated to the application of the fertilizer have to be calculated in the crop production activity.
Image: https://db3.ecoinvent.org/images/e8342b95-78fd-4a83-a444-47e7464508b4
Technology:  Inventory based on composition of ''thomas meal''.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This dataset was created as a weighted average (by production volumes) of the regional clinker production activities.
The dataset describes the production of portland cement clinker; in the production different types of alternative fuels and raw materials are used.
Clinker is the active component of Portland cement that, once mixed with water, contributes to the hardening of the cement itself. As a result, clinker plays a fundamental role in the production of various construction materials, including cements, concretes, and mortars. The manufacturing of clinker is highly energy-intensive and requires a significant amount of raw materials.
Large amounts of limestone, along with additional raw materials like sand, clay, iron ore, and other corrective materials—roughly 1.7 tons of these materials per ton of clinker—are thermochemically processed in order to produce clinker. Kiln ovens, as well as associated infrastructure and equipment, are needed for this process. The kiln oven must maintain high temperatures because alite, a crucial mineral in the composition of clinker that gives concrete most of its initial strength after hydration, must form at a temperature of roughly 1450°C. In order to maintain these high temperatures, a significant amount of secondary fuels (wastes) as well as conventional fuels are used to produce this energy.
The kiln technology and fuel mix are highly dependent on geographical factors.
Geography:  Activity created as a weighted average (by production volume) of the regional clinker production activities (IN, Europe without Switzerland, US, BR, ZA, CA, CO, PE, TN, EC and CH).
Technology:  Clinker manufacturing begins with the collection and transportation of raw materials to the production site. The raw materials are crushed, mixed, and milled into a raw meal, which is then processed in a kiln oven. Depending on available technologies, the kiln can be either a wet or a long dry type, with the latter requiring lower energy input. Current technologies may include preheaters and precalciners, further reducing energy requirements before the raw meal enters the dry kiln.
The principal chemical reactions during pyroprocessing for clinker production are:
CaCO3 => CaO + CO2 (800°C–900°C)
2CaO + SiO2 => (CaO)2⋅SiO2 (800°C–900°C)
3CaO + Al2O3 => (CaO)3⋅Al2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
4CaO + Al2O3 + Fe2O3 => (CaO)4⋅Al2O3⋅Fe2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
CaO + (CaO)2.SiO2 => (CaO)3⋅SiO2 (>1300°C)
Following pyroprocessing in the kiln, rapid cooling is necessary to preserve the high-temperature phase assemblage, which is thermodynamically unstable at intermediate temperatures but metastably preserved at lower temperatures due to energy and mixing barriers.
Time period:  The dataset represents a weighted average of the regional activities. The time period is set according to the time period of the regional activities.
classifications: 3928: Sawdust and wood waste and scrap
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
Clinker - Activity starts with reception of raw material at factory gate and its internal transportation for prehomogenisation and raw meal grinding where different raw materials are mixed to obtain the chemical composition required for cement. The homogenized raw meal then fed to preheater tower to preheat the feed before entering to precalciner & kiln. A pre-heater is a series of vertical cyclones through which the raw meal is passed, coming into contact with swirling hot gases moving in the opposite direction. Precalcination takes place in precalciner where decomposition of limestone to lime take place. The required reactions, which also need heat energy inputs, are stimulated at two points in the manufacturing process: within the “precalciner”, a combustion chamber at the bottom of the pre-heater above the kiln, and within the kiln itself. The precalcined meal then enters the kiln, where intense heat – up to 1450°C – causes chemical and physical reactions that partially melt the meal into “clinker”, an intermediate product in cement manufacturing that becomes the main substance in cement and is commonly traded. From the kiln, the hot clinker falls onto a grate cooler where it is cooled by incoming combustion air, thereby minimising energy loss from the system. Depending on the requirement clinker can be stored in the silo to use for making different type of cement or transported to different location.  Total clinker production in india during the year of 2014-15 is approximately 190 million tonnes. Clinker is basic raw material for production of cement.
Geography:  Data represents for all geographical locations where cement has been manufactured in India.
Technology:  For this activity latest available technologies were considered like Vertical roller mill, energy efficient fans, use of efficienct burner and cooler etc.
Time period:  For this activity time period of 2014 to 2017 has been considered . As during this period Indian cement industry has done significant works in improving energy efficiency by installing energy efficient equipment, process optimization, equipment retrofit etc.
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production

For special cements, such as low heat (LH) and sulfate resistant (SR) types, it is necessary to limit the amount of tricalcium aluminate formed. The major raw material for the clinker-making is usually limestone mixed with a second material containing clay as source of alumino-silicate. Normally, an impure limestone which contains clay or silicon dioxide (SiO2) is used. The calcium carbonate (CaCO3) content of these limestones can be as low as 80%. Second raw materials (materials in the raw mix other than limestone) depend on the purity of the limestone. Some of the second raw materials used are: clay, shale, sand, iron ore, and bauxite.
Time period:  Time period corresponds to data collection (2017-2021).

The dataset describes the production of cement (CEM II/A) in Switzerland and covers the representative production mix of CEM II/A-D, CEM II/A-LL, CEM II/A-M(D-LL), CEM II/A-M(V-LL) and CEM II/A-S according to SN EN 197-1.
In Switzerland, according to the Swiss standard SN EN 197-1:2011, cement CEM II, also known as Portland composite cement, has 65-94% of clinker and is mixed with different materials, such as limestone, ground granulated blast furnace slag, fly ash, pozzolan, burnt shale, silica fume, among others.
Geography:  This dataset represents production of cement CEM II/A for Switzerland.
Technology:  In this dataset, the production of CEM II/A is described, where clinker (77.7%) is ground together with gypsum (4.9%) and mixed with limestone (15%), silica fume, blast furnace slag and ground granulated blast furnace slag (GGBFS). The activity includes also the input of electricity used for the grinding of the clinker, grinding aids (ethylene glycol and iron sulfate) and heat (heavy fuel oil) for the drying of additions.
Time period:  Time period corresponds to data collection (2017-2021).

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This dataset was created as a weighted average (by production volumes) of the regional clinker production activities.
The dataset describes the production of portland cement clinker; in the production different types of alternative fuels and raw materials are used.
Clinker is the active component of Portland cement that, once mixed with water, contributes to the hardening of the cement itself. As a result, clinker plays a fundamental role in the production of various construction materials, including cements, concretes, and mortars. The manufacturing of clinker is highly energy-intensive and requires a significant amount of raw materials.
Large amounts of limestone, along with additional raw materials like sand, clay, iron ore, and other corrective materials—roughly 1.7 tons of these materials per ton of clinker—are thermochemically processed in order to produce clinker. Kiln ovens, as well as associated infrastructure and equipment, are needed for this process. The kiln oven must maintain high temperatures because alite, a crucial mineral in the composition of clinker that gives concrete most of its initial strength after hydration, must form at a temperature of roughly 1450°C. In order to maintain these high temperatures, a significant amount of secondary fuels (wastes) as well as conventional fuels are used to produce this energy.
The kiln technology and fuel mix are highly dependent on geographical factors.
Geography:  Activity created as a weighted average (by production volume) of the regional clinker production activities (IN, Europe without Switzerland, US, BR, ZA, CA, CO, PE, TN, EC and CH).
Technology:  Clinker manufacturing begins with the collection and transportation of raw materials to the production site. The raw materials are crushed, mixed, and milled into a raw meal, which is then processed in a kiln oven. Depending on available technologies, the kiln can be either a wet or a long dry type, with the latter requiring lower energy input. Current technologies may include preheaters and precalciners, further reducing energy requirements before the raw meal enters the dry kiln.
The principal chemical reactions during pyroprocessing for clinker production are:
CaCO3 => CaO + CO2 (800°C–900°C)
2CaO + SiO2 => (CaO)2⋅SiO2 (800°C–900°C)
3CaO + Al2O3 => (CaO)3⋅Al2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
4CaO + Al2O3 + Fe2O3 => (CaO)4⋅Al2O3⋅Fe2O3 (900°C–1300°C)
CaO + (CaO)2.SiO2 => (CaO)3⋅SiO2 (>1300°C)
Following pyroprocessing in the kiln, rapid cooling is necessary to preserve the high-temperature phase assemblage, which is thermodynamically unstable at intermediate temperatures but metastably preserved at lower temperatures due to energy and mixing barriers.
Time period:  The dataset represents a weighted average of the regional activities. The time period is set according to the time period of the regional activities.
classifications: 39950: Wastes from chemical or allied industries
ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.name: clinker production

As a result, this dataset covers 90% of the national production of grey clinker manufacturing in Canada. Therefore, this dataset can be considered highly representative of the declared geographical scope, with no need for further geographical extrapolation.
The geographical overlap between Canada and Canada-Ontario for 'clinker production' is due to the high representativity of the collected data, making both datasets highly geographically representative. As a result, both datasets have been reported, with the 'Canada' dataset serving as the national average.
Time period:  The dataset covers grey cement clinker production in Canada, for the year 2020. It aggregates data from 13 plants, averaging exchanges across various kiln technology mixes and production volumes from different suppliers. Therefore, since the reported exchanges and technology level are not anticipated to fluctuate significantly over time, the dataset has high temporal representativeness.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
The dataset describes the production of Portland cement clinker in Tunisia. 
Clinker is the main constituent of Portland cement and also the main responsible for its environmental impacts. Clinker is made by sintering a mixture composed mainly of limestone and clay (called “raw meal”) at temperatures up to 1450 °C. Both primary and secondary fuels are used to this end. After sintering, clinker nodules have a diameter between 5 mm and 25 mm.
Clinker consists of various calcium silicates including alite (3CaO.SiO2 or “C3S”) and belite (2CaO.SiO2 or “C2S”). Tricalcium aluminate (3CaO.Al2O3 or “C3A”) and calcium aluminoferrite (4CaO.Al2O3.Fe2O3 or “C4AF”) are other common components, added to reduce the sintering temperature of the calcium silicates. For special cement types, such as low heat and sulfate resistant, it is necessary to limit the amount of tricalcium aluminate formed.
Pyroprocessing, and cement preparation: - Raw material extraction - Crushing of raw material - Pre-homogenization and raw meal grinding
 - Pre-calcining - Production of clinker - Cooling and storage - Clinker grinding and the cement manufacturing process - Milling of cement
After the kiln, clinker is cooled and stored in silos. Clinker production plants are usually integrated with cement plants.
Primary data were gathered from one cement manufacturer in Tunisia who informed the total values of inputs and outputs related to clinker production in 2019 considering all their clinker production sites. All types of flows were reported, namely: primary and secondary raw materials and fuels, auxiliary materials, electricity consumption, water consumption and air emissions (from emissions monitoring reports). It corresponds to 15% of the market share, considered a good representativeness level.
SOURCES:
 Schorcht, F., Kourti, I., Scalet, B.M., Roudier, S., Sancho, L.D., 2013. Best available techniques (BAT) reference document for the production of cement, lime and magnesium oxide. Eur. Comm. Jt. Res. Cent. Inst. Prospect. Technol. Stud. Rep. EUR 26129 EN Luxemb. Publ. Off. Eur. Union
Boesch, M.E., Koehler, A., Hellweg, S., 2009. Model for Cradle-to-Gate Life Cycle Assessment of Clinker Production. Environ. Sci. Technol. 43, 7578–7583. https://doi.org/10.1021/es900036e
Image: https://db3.ecoinvent.org/images/bd0062b7-f504-4b77-b43a-3a83c12158e4
Image: https://db3.ecoinvent.org/images/4015964a-30e6-4523-a434-066662163063
Geography:  This dataset represents production of clinker for Tunisia. Data come from one cement manufacturer in Tunisia.
Technology:  Current technology for clinker production in Tunisian dry kilns, considering various fuel types.
In Tunisia, cement plants use dry kilns with preheater and precalciner, being a total of nine cement plants (eight gray clinker and one white clinker) (dry). This dataset represents data for gray clinker from one cement manufacturer. Regarding fuel types, 88,1% are fossil fuels (petroleum coke), 11% biomass (olive poumace) and 0,9% natural gas (data from 2019).
Sources: 
Single Market Profile: TUNISIA 2022. 
Institut National de la Statistique. Statistiques Tunisie. BULLETIN MENSUEL DES STATISTIQUES. Janvier 2020
Time period:  The dataset represents a cement plant in Tunisia. The time period is set according to the historical data (2019) collected from the industry.
Data correspond to 2019.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
Pulverized fuel ash-lime bricks are obtained from materials consisting of pulverized fuel ash in major quantity, lime and an accelerator acting as a catalyst. Pulverized fuel ash-lime bricks are generally manufactured by intergrading blending various raw materials are then moulded into bricks and subjected to curing cycles at different temperatures and pressures. These bricks are suitable for use in masonry construction just like common burnt clay bricks.
Geography:  Data represents for southern part of India,
Technology:  Fly ash, Lime, Gypsum and Sand are manually feed into a pan mixer where water is added to the required proportion for homogeneous mixing. The proportion of raw material may vary depending upon quality of raw materials. After mixing, the mixture are allowed to belt conveyor through feed in to automatic brick making machine were the bricks are pressed automatically. Then the bricks are placed on wooden pallets and kept as it is for two days thereafter transported to open area where they are water cured for 10 -15 days. The bricks are sorted and tested before dispatch.
Time period:  Data available for these period.

ecoinvent-3.12-consequential.comment:
This dataset contains the production of 20 MPa ready-mix concrete used for building construction with Portland cement. Density: 2'335 kg/m³. Ingredients (for 1 m³): Cement 204 kg, Water 160 kg, Gravel 1009 kg, Sand 925 kg, Fly ash 36.38 kg, Admixtures (air-entrainers and superplastisizers) 1,2kg. 20 MPa concrete is intended for residential use, such as footings for walls, columns, fireplaces, chimneys, foundations, walls, grade beams, piers, not exposed to freeze-thaw. (following the Canadian CSA specs - http://www.rmcao.org/sites/default/files/CSA%20Quickspec%202012.pdf) Generic concrete mixes (cement, SCM, aggregate, water) provided by the CPCA (Athena (2005):Cement and Structural Concrete: LCI Update 2005; Table 10.1). Admixtures included in the mixes, as provided by the CPCI (Athena and Morrison Hershfield (2012): Life Cycle Assessment of Precast Concrete Commercial Buildings Cradle-to-Grave; Table 5). The average water consumption (not including batch water) is 65 L/m³ of ready mixed concrete. The average amount of water disposed is 35 L/m³. Water not discarded is recycled back into the process or into other products, and therefore not accounted for in this dataset.
Geography:  North American data. For some exchanges RER-modules have been used as proxy. Data quality indicators adjusted accordingly.
Technology:  Typical technology for average North American production. Data quality indicators adjusted accordingly.
